title: allsol2901
body:
UNSW School of Mathematics and Statistics MATH2901 Higher Theory of Statistics T UTORIAL EXERCISES - S OLUTIONS

Contents 1 Randomness, Variability, Uncertainty, Probability 2

2 Elements of Probability theory 7

3 Random variables 16

4 Random vectors 28

5 Common distributions 40

6 Transformations of random variables 51

7 Introduction to statistical inference 61

8 Estimation methods 67

9 Confidence intervals 80

10 Hypothesis testing 87  1 Randomness, Variability, Uncertainty, Probability 1.1. (a) Before the coin lands, the outcome has not yet been determined. Given that there are two possible results (sample space = {Heads, Tails}) and no inherent bias favouring one over the other (assuming a fair coin and an ideal flip), the outcome is decided totally at random. Consequently, each outcome must be assigned an equal probability, meaning the probability of landing on Heads must be 1/2 as well as that of landing in Tails (; ‘distribution’). These probabilities correspond to the long-run relative frequencies of Heads and Tails: if the coin was flipped a large number of times, it would land on Heads approximately half the time, and on Tails the other half. (b) As reminded above, randomness is defined in relation to the existence of a distribution, which describes the overall pattern of all possible outcomes of an experiment. Therefore, the concept of randomness is meaningful only when considering possible outcomes yet to come, but no more after the experiment has taken place and the outcome determined. At that stage, we are past the point where randomness is relevant: the unpredictability has been resolved.

Here, once the coin has landed, the outcome is already determined: it is either Heads or Tails, and there is no longer any randomness at play. As a result, the only meaningful value we could give to the probability of observing Heads would be: 0 if the coin landed on Tails and 1 if it landed on Heads – but we do not know which!

It is true that we are still uncertain about the outcome, but this stems from a lack of knowledge, not from randomness itself. This is an example of epistemic uncertainty, which arises from incomplete information, as opposed to aleatory uncertainty, which is due to inherent randomness in the process like in part (a).

Whether or not we are still willing to say: ‘there is a probability of 1/2 that the coin under your hand shows Heads’, is largely a philosophical question. There are two main perspectives on this issue:

• The frequentist perspective: a probability is strictly tied to the long-run frequency of occurrence of an outcome (like described in part (a)). From this viewpoint, once the experiment has been conducted and the outcome determined, the sample space of possible outcomes collapses to the single outcome that has actually occurred! Since the notion of a ‘frequency of occurrence’ for different possible outcomes is no longer meaningful in this context, it is not appropriate to assign probabilities other than the trivial values 0 or 1. In particular, a statement like ‘there is a probability of 1/2 that the coin under your hand shows Heads’ violates the frequentist view.

The only valid statement we can make is that, had this coin flip been part of a large number of similar flips, approximately half would have resulted in Heads. In that sense, there is no reason to believe more strongly that Heads lies under you hand, than Tails. We usually convey this idea by saying that we can be as ‘confident’ that Heads is under your hand as we are that Tails is. This level of confidence (here:

50%) is what is typically reported; indeed, simply stating ‘the probability is either 0 or 1 but we do not know which’ would not sound very serious. • The Bayesian perspective: the idea that our realisation of the experiment is only one instance in a large series of hypothetical identical repetitions is rejected, so the ‘long-run frequency of occurrence’ interpretation of a probability becomes null and void. Instead, a probability is interpreted as a measure of degree of belief in a given statement. From this viewpoint, a probability need not reflect inherent randomness but can instead represent a state of incomplete information. In our scenario, although the coin’s outcome has already been determined, we do not yet know it. Thus, we can still assign a probability 1/2 to the event that the coin under your hand shows Heads, as this quantifies our uncertainty based on the information available.

Now, it can be understood that the distinction between aleatory uncertainty (due to intrinsic randomness) and epistemic uncertainty (due to lack of knowledge) is often quite blurry. For example, we can argue that, in all that matters, the two situations considered in (a) and (b) are effectively identical: since we have no knowledge of what lies under your hand, we could just as well ignore that trial, re-flip the coin and only record the outcome at that point.

Also, the randomness which seems inherent in the process of flipping a coin may arise largely from our ignorance of the precise physical conditions governing the flip. In principle, if we knew the exact initial velocity, angular momentum, air resistance, surface properties, and even minute factors like the coin’s deformation upon impact or slight variations in air currents, we could predict the outcome deterministically – but of course, there is no way we could ever know about all that!

This idea aligns with the concept of Laplace’s Demon, a thought experiment proposed by Pierre-Simon Laplace in 1814. He imagined an all-knowing intellect (said demon) that, given complete knowledge of the position and momentum of every particle in the universe, could predict the future with absolute certainty. From this perspective, randomness is merely an illusion created by our limited knowledge rather than an inherent feature of reality. In other words, there is no such thing as ‘aleatory uncertainty’, only ‘epistemic uncertainty’. While this view may have some appeal in theory, it is easy to think of counter-examples.

Finally, let us clarify that the difference between frequentist and Bayesian approaches is mostly a matter of interpretation. Regardless of the viewpoint one adopts, the fundamental mathematical framework developed in the following chapters (probability rules, distributions, statistical methods, etc.) remains the same. To quote the American mathematician and statistician Leonard Savage in ‘The foundations of Statistics’ (1954):

‘It is unanimously agreed that statistics depends somehow on probability. But, as to what probability is and how it is connected with statistics, there has seldom been such complete disagreement and breakdown of communication since the Tower of Babel. Doubtless, much of the disagreement is merely terminological and would disappear under sufficiently sharp analysis.’

In this class, we will take a frequentist perspective, as is standard in probability courses at this level, interpreting probabilities in terms of long-run frequencies. However, we will not be overly rigid in our approach and may occasionally refer to probabilities in contexts where uncertainty arises from incomplete information rather than inherent randomness (see Question 1.3 below). This will not affect mathematical rigour at this stage, so there is no need to dwell on the philosophical distinction too much – simply being aware of it is sufficient. To go further, the UNSW class MATH3871 is entirely devoted to Bayesian Probability and Statistics. 1.2. (a) Let us suppose that there are l million (1M) people in the population (any ‘large’ number would do). Suppose that they are all tested. Then, amongst the 1M people, about 1M × 99% = 990,000 (990K) would not have the disease, of whom about (1M × 99%) × 10% = 99K would test positive. Also, about 1M × 1% = 10K would have the disease, of whom about (1M × 1%) × 90% = 9K would test positive.

So, over the whole population, a total of about 99K + 9K = 108K would test positive, of whom only 9K would actually have the disease. In other words, only 9/108 = 1/12 of those who would test positive actually have the disease. A random person drawn from the population and testing positive is actually much more likely not to have the disease than otherwise. (b) This is akin to the problem studied in 1.1b above. Here there is no ‘randomness’ as such in whether or not Homer has the disease: either he does have it or he does not have it – it is just that we do not know. From a frequentist perspective, all that we can say to Homer is that if every person were tested, then the fraction of those with positive results who would have the disease is 1/12; and in this sense he can be 11/12 (≈ 91.7%) ‘confident’ that he does not have the disease.

This said, in this class, we will not be overly hesitant to state that Homer has the disease with probability 1/12 = 8.3% (I’ll make sure to send him a copy of the lecture notes so he can decide what that statement means to him!) 1.3. (a) Since each of the 1,000 pages is equally likely to be removed (‘tear out one page totally at random’), the probability that page 42 is the one missing is simply 1/1, 000 = 0.001. (b) Yes, this does change the probability that page 42 is missing. Learning that page 500 is present rules out the possibility that page 500 was removed. It does not make any other page more or less likely to be missing, but instead of 1,000 possibilities initially, we have now ‘only’ 999 left. So, the probability that page 42 is now missing is 1/999 = 0.001001. In fact, what we have here is a case of conditional probability: the obtained probability of page 4 being the missing one is valid if page 500 is not the one which has been removed – i.e., under a given condition. (c) Now, the friend has ruled out 500 pages, leaving only the remaining 500 (including page 42) as possibilities for the missing page. Since every page between 1 and 500 was

equally likely to be removed at the beginning, and that remains true, the probability that page 42 is missing is now 1/500 = 0.002. Again, this is a conditional probability. (d) As discussed earlier, in a strictly frequentist sense, we should refrain from using the term ‘probability’ for events that have already occurred but are simply unknown to us (such as which page has been torn off). Instead, we would speak in terms of confidence levels. From this perspective, stating that ‘there is a probability of 0.001 that page 42 is missing’ in (a) would not be strictly meaningful. However, here we will take a more flexible approach and allow the use of such terminology.

Conditional probability presents an interesting hybrid case. Once we condition on a known event (such as ‘it is not page 500 which is missing’ or ‘it is not a page between 501 and 1000 which is missing’ in (b) and (c)), we have effectively ruled out certain possible outcomes. Yet, in general, more than one outcome remains possible, meaning that we are still left with a non-trivial sample space of remaining possibilities, on which we may imagine a distribution in the frequentist sense. For example, if we were to repeat the process ‘tearing a page totally at random’ a large number of time and keep only the cases where the torn page is not page 500, then the long-run frequency of times that the torn page would be page 42 is 1/999.

In this sense, conditional probability acts as a bridge between the frequentist and Bayesian perspectives: it retains the frequentist foundation by being defined in terms of limiting relative frequencies, yet it also allows us to update our degree of belief in an event after conditioning on partial knowledge in what resembles the Bayesian way. 1.4. (a) The coin is fair, so the probability of Heads or Tails on any given flip is 50% – and this remains true regardless of past results. The fact that those probabilities remain unaffected by previous flips is referred to as independence, which is an essential concept in Probability (see Section 2.9). Concretely: the coin does not magically turn into a biased coin at some point, it has no memory and does not attempt to ‘correct’ itself. The misconception arises from misunderstanding randomness: the distribution captures the long-run frequency of occurrence of Heads and Tails, and by long-run we really mean ‘in the limit, over an infinite number of repetitions of the experiment’. What happens over any finite number of such repetitions cannot alter that limit. (b) Another common example of the gambler’s fallacy occurs in lottery numbers. Suppose a particular set of numbers (e.g., 5-12-23-34-45-49) has not been drawn in a national lottery for years. Some people might believe that this combination is now ‘due’ to appear soon, thinking that the longer a number hasn’t appeared, the more likely it is to be drawn. However, each lottery draw is independent, meaning that past outcomes do not influence future ones. The probability of any specific number combination remains the same in every draw, regardless of previous results.

In financial markets, investors sometimes believe that if a stock has been falling for several days in a row, it is ‘due’ for a rebound. In reality, stock price movements are influenced by market conditions, news, and economic factors, not by the idea that the stock must go up after a losing streak. Assuming a stock will rise just because it has

been declining is a classic example of the gambler’s fallacy; in this case, also known as the ‘mean-reversion fallacy’.

Many sports fans may believe that a basketball player who has missed several free throws in a row is ‘due’ to make the next one. In reality, unless there is an underlying change (such as player fatigue, or other performance-related factors such as loss of confidence), each shot is independent of the previous ones. The player’s probability of making the next free throw remains the same as before (assuming no external influences). (c) Mathematically, the probability remains 50% for each outcome, assuming the coin is fair. However, here our intuition may suggest bias in the coin after such an extreme streak. Concretely, such a streak would be so unlikely to occur if the coin was fair, then one might suspect the coin to be unfair and consider statistical tests to assess the extent of the bias – we’ll study such statistical hypothesis tests in Chapter 10. This is obviously a totally different question that those related to the gambler’s fallacy.

1.5. (a) Variability in height measurements arises from genetic differences, environmental factors (e.g., nutrition), and measurement errors. When randomly selecting groups, different individuals will be chosen each time, leading to natural variation in the observed heights. Thus, we do not expect to see the same values in every sample. (b) A 70% chance of rain for tomorrow means that it would rain on 70% of the days exposed to identical atmospheric conditions (which the forecast model accounts for). It does not mean that it will definitely rain or that rain is ‘due’. A correct forecast does not guarantee a specific outcome: if it does not rain, the forecast was not wrong; it simply happened that tomorrow was one of those 30% of like days on which it was not to rain! This just reflects uncertainty. (c) The percentage may fluctuate due to sampling variability, as different individuals are selected each time. Increasing the sample size reduces this variability, making the observed proportion more stable and aligning it more closely with the true population proportion, simply because a larger sample better covers the entire population. This is known as the Law of Large Numbers, and will be discussed in Chapter 4.

2 Elements of Probability theory 2.1. (a) A ∩ B c ∩ C c (b) A ∪ B ∪ C (c) (A ∩ B) ∪ (A ∩ C) ∪ (B ∩ C) (d) (A ∩ B c ∩ C c ) ∪ (Ac ∩ B ∩ C c ) ∪ (Ac ∩ B c ∩ C) (e) (A ∩ B ∩ C c ) ∪ (A ∩ B c ∩ C) ∪ (Ac ∩ B ∩ C) 2.2. 2.3. (a) If E1 is independent of itself, then P(E1 ∩ E1 ) = P(E1 ) × P(E1 ). As (E1 ∩ E1 ) ≡ E1 , this reduces to P(E1 ) = (P(E1 ))2 , so that P(E1 ) can only be 0 or 1. (b) If P(E1 ) = 0, then for any other event E2 , 0 ≤ P(E1 ∩ E2 ) ≤ P(E1 ) = 0, so P(E1 ∩ E2 ) = 0 also. Thus, P(E1 ∩ E2 ) = 0 × P(E2 ) = P(E1 ) × P(E2 ), and E1 and E2 are independent. For any E2 , P(E1 ∩ E2 ) = P(E2 ) − P(E1c ∩ E2 ). But if P(E1 ) = 1, P(E1c ) = 0 and 0 ≤ P(E1c ∩ E2 ) ≤ P(E1c ) = 0, implying P(E1 ∩ E2 ) = P(E2 ). Hence P(E1 ∩ E2 ) = 1 × P(E2 ) = P(E1 ) × P(E2 ). E1 and E2 are independent. 2.4. Clearly, P(E1c ∩ E2 ) = P(E2 ) − P(E1 ∩ E2 ) = P(E2 ) − P(E1 )P(E2 ) = P(E2 )(1 − P(E1 )) = P(E1c )P(E2 )

; E1c and E2 are independent. For the second part, apply the first part to the pair of events E2 , E1c .

2.5. From Bayes’ first rule we can write P(E1 |E2 ) P(E2 |E1 ) = . P(E1 ) P(E2 ) Hence, if the left-hand side is greater than 1, the right-hand side must be so.

2.6. Let M denote the event “the randomly chosen worker is married” and G denote “the randomly chosen worker is a university graduate”. We are given

P(M ) = 0.72, P(G) = 0.44, P(M |G) = 0.5.

(a) We have:

P(neither married nor a university graduate) = P(M c ∩ Gc ) = P((M ∪ G)c ) = 1 − P(M ∪ G) = 1 − {P(M ) + P(G) − P(M ∩ G)} = 1 − {P(M ) + P(G) − P(M |G)P(G)} = 1 − (0.72 + 0.44 − 21 × 0.44) = 0.06

(b)

P(married but not a university graduate) = P(M ∩ Gc ) = 1 − P((M ∩ Gc )c ) = 1 − P(M c ∪ G) = 1 − {P(M c ) + P(G) − P(M c ∩ G)} = 1 − {P(M c ) + P(G) − P(M c |G)P(G)} = 1 − (0.28 + 0.44 − 0.5 × 0.44) = 0.5

(c)

P(married or a university student) = P(M ∪ G) = 1 − P(M c ∩ Gc ) = 1 − 0.06 = 0.94.

2.7. Let R be the event that a person is promoted, and M be the event that they are male. For a randomly selected worker, we are given:

P(R ∩ M ) = 0.17 P(Rc ∩ M ) = 0.68 P(M ) = 0.85 P(R ∩ M c ) = 0.03 P(Rc ∩ M c ) = 0.12 P(M c ) = 0.15

Now using this information,

P(R ∩ M ) 0.17 P(R|M ) = = = 0.2 P(M ) 0.85

while P(R) = P(R ∩ M ) + P(R ∩ M c ) = 0.17 + 0.03 = 0.2. Therefore, R and M are independent (P(R|M ) = P(R)); and there is no gender bias in promotion at that workplace.

2.8. We require: P(Product A ∩ Local) = P(Product A) · P(Local). Call x the number of customers who must have purchased Product B from the online store. Then we have: 4 10 10 P(Product A ∩ Local) = , P(Product A) = , P(Local) = 16 + x 16 + x 16 + x Solving the equation 4 100 = 16 + x (16 + x)2 yields 4(16 + x) = 100, that is, x = 9.

2.9. Note that A can win only at odd turns, while B can win only at even turns. We have ∞ X P(A wins) = P (A wins at the (2k + 1)th turn) k=0 ∞ X = (1 − p)2k p k=0 ∞ ∞ X 2 k X 1 xk =  =p (1 − p) (using for |x| < 1) k=0 k=0 1−x p = 1 − (1 − p)2 = 2−p

; P(B wins) = 1 − P(A wins) = 1 − 2−p = 1−p 2−p

p Note that P(A wins) − P(B wins) = 2−p , which is always positive regardless of the value of p. This reflects the inherent advantage of starting the game, an advantage that becomes even more pronounced as p increases (why?).

2.10. Let trial i consist of the (2i − 1)th and the (2i)th points. So, trial 1 consists of the first two points, trial 2 of the next two points, and so on. During each trial, two things can happen: either a player (A or B) wins the two points, and the game is over, or each player wins one point, and the game starts again from the initial situation. The former happens with probability p2 + (1 − p)2 , while the latter happens with probability 2p(1 − p). A total of 2n points (i.e., n trials) are played if the first (n − 1) trials all result in each player winning one point, and then one player winning both points during the nth trial. By independence,

P(2n points are needed) = (2p(1 − p))n−1 (p2 + (1 − p)2 )

P(A wins on trial n) = (2p(1 − p))n−1 p2 and ∞ X P(A wins) = P(A wins on trial n) n=1 ∞ X =p (2p(1 − p))n−1 n=1 ∞ X = p2 (2p(1 − p))n n=0 p2 = , 1 − 2p(1 − p)

using the identity ∞ X 1 xk = k=0 1−x provided |x| < 1. It also follows

(1 − p)2 P(B wins) = 1 − P(A wins) = 1 − 2p(1 − p)

2.11. On each toss, the sum of the faces is 3 with probability 2/36, 7 with probability 6/36 and neither 3 nor 7 with probability 1 − 2/36 − 6/36 = 28/36.

We will obtain a sum of 3 before a sum of 7 on the kth toss if none of the first (k − 1) tosses gave a sum of 3 or 7, and the nth toss gives a sum of 3. Altogether, we will obtain a sum of 3 before a sum of 7 with probability ∞  k−1 X 28 2 2 1 2 1 2 1 = = = = , k=1 36 36 36 1 − 28/36 36 8/36 8 4 P∞ k 1 where again we have used k=0 x = 1−x for |x| < 1.

2.13. (a) Let A be the event “Urn 1 is selected” and B be the event “ball is red”. Then A and Ac partition the sample space and by the Law of Total Probability:

P(B) = P(B|A)P(A) + P(B|Ac )P(Ac ) = 25 × 12 + 49 × 12 = 19

(b) Again we appeal to the Law of Total Probability and argue more directly (without introducing A and B notation):

P(second red) = P(second red|first red)P(first red) +P(second red|first black)P(first black) = 10 × 52 + 10 5 4 × 53 = 11

(c) Let A be the event “first black” and B be the event “second red”. By Bayes’ Formula: P(B|A)P(A) ×3 6 P(A|B) = = 10 11 5 = 11 . P(B) 25

2.14. Let T be the event that the blood test returns a positive result. Let D be the event of a Down’s Syndrome baby. We are given: P(D) = P(T |D) = 0.89 P(T c |Dc ) = 0.75   (a) From the Law of Total Probability,

P(T ) = P(T |D)P(D) + P(T |Dc )P(Dc ) 1 269 = 0.89 × + (1 − 0.75) × 270 270 ≈ 0.0033 + 0.2491 = 0.2524

(b) From Baye’s Formula,

P(T |D)P(D) P(D|T ) = P(T ) 0.89 × 270 ≈ 0.2524 ≈ 0.013

(c)

P(T c |D)P(D) P(D|T c ) = P(T c ) (1 − 0.89) × 270 ≈ (1 − 0.2524) ≈ 0.00054 ≈

2.15. Let T = {Driver tested}, D = {Driver is drunk}. We are given:

P(T |D) = 0.8, P(T c |Dc ) = 0.8, P(D) = 0.12.

(a) P(T |Dc ) = 1 − P(T c |Dc ) = 1 − 0.8 = 0.2. (b) By the Law of Total Probability:

P(T ) = P(T |D)P(D) + P(T |Dc )P(Dc ) = 0.8 × 0.12 + 0.2 × 0.88 = 0.272

(c) By Bayes’ first rule:

P(D) 0.12 P(D|T ) = P(T |D) = 0.8 × = 0.353 P(T ) 0.272

(d) By Bayes’ first rule again:

P(D) 0.12 P(D|T c ) = P(T c |D) c = (1 − 0.8) × = 0.033. P(T ) 1 − 0.272

2.16. (a) There are three prisoners: you, A and B. Define the events Y /A/B as “you/A/B is chosen to be hanged”. See that P(Y ) = P(A) = P(B) = 13 (prior probability of being hanged).

Now, denote JY /JA /JB the events “the jailer says that you/A/B will be set free”. By Bayes’ first rule, P(Y ) P(Y |JA ) = P(JA |Y ) . P(JA ) If you are chosen to be hanged, it is reasonable to suppose that the answer of the jailer is equally likely to be A or B, that is, P(JA |Y ) = P(JB |Y ) = . (⋆) Also, P(JA |A) = 0 (because the jailer will not say that A is going to be set free if he’s the one to be hanged) and P(JA |B) = 1 (because the jailer will not say that you are going to be set free). So, by the law of total probability, we have P(JA ) = P(JA |A)P(A) + P(JA |B)P(B) + P(JA |Y )P(Y ) = 0 + 1 × 1/3 + 1/2 × 1/3 = 1/3 1 P(Y |JA ) = 1/2 × = , 1/2 3 so that P(Y |JA ) = P(Y ). Similarly, we could find P(Y |JB ) = P(Y ), so that you’ll be hanged or not independently of the answer of the jailer. 1/3 P(B) (b) P(B|JA ) = P(JA |B) P(J A) = 1 × 1/2 = 23 , to be compared with ‘your’ P(Y |JA ) = 1/3

; you’d better not take his identity. (In fact, the previous answers (a) and (b) depend critically on the assumption (⋆). Suppose that, if you are the one chosen to be hanged, the jailer always tells you that A will be set free, so that P(JA |Y ) = 1 and P(JB |Y ) = 0. How does this affect the previous answer? This problem has become known as the Three Prisoners paradox, or sometimes also the Monty Hall problem.) 2.17. (a) P(system fails) = P(at least one component fails) = 1 − P(no components fail) = 1 − (1 − p)n (by independence)

(b) P(system fails) = P(all components fail) = pn (by independence)

2.18. (a) The probability is 1 if n ≥ 366 (of course). Assume n ≤ 366. As all days are equally likely to be somebody’s birthday, we may use the classical definition of probability: pn = P(at least two people have a common birthday) = 1 − P(none have a common birthday) 365 × 364 × × {365 − (n − 1)} =1− 365n 364 × 363 × × (365 − n + 1) =1− 365n−1

for a fixed n. You may plot this as a function of n; this is an increasing function of n (of course!), with p1 = 0 (of course...) and reaching 1 at n = 366. (b) Take n = 23 in the above expression, and get p23 = 0.507. This is the smallest value of n which makes pn > 1/2. So, as soon as there are 23 or more persons in a room, it is more likely than otherwise to have two with the same birthday. (c) Call X the number of people you need to ask until you find someone sharing your birthday. Then,  n−1 364 1 P(X = n) = 365 365 hence n−1  i n n 1 1 − 364  X 364 1 365 364 P(X ≤ n) = = =1− , i=0 365 365 365 1 − 364365 n+1 where we have used the fact that, for N < ∞, nk=0 xk = 1−x P 1−x for x ̸= 1. If we want P(X ≤ n) = 0.5:  n 0.5 = 1 −  n 0.5 = log(0.5) n = 364  ≃ 252.7 log 365 ; we need to ask at least 253 people. 2.19. Let Hi /Ti denote the events “the coin shows up heads/tails on the ith flip”. You can ignore the flips which don’t allow you to choose an alternative (when two successive flips give the same result): they are simply discarded, and the process restarts. So, conditioning on the fact the two flips gave different results, the probability of choosing alternative 1 is given by P(H2 ∩ T1 ) p(1 − p) 1 P((H2 ∩T1 )|(H1 ∩T2 )∪(H2 ∩T1 )) = = = P((H1 ∩ T2 ) ∪ (H2 ∩ T1 )) p(1 − p) + p(1 − p) 2 for all p ∈ (0, 1).

2.20. Following the hint, we can write

P(X + Y = 2) = P(X = 1) × P(Y = 1) = f1 g1 P(X + Y = 12) = P(X = 6) × P(Y = 6) = f6 g6 P(X + Y = 7) ≥ P(X = 1) × P(X = 6) + P(X = 6) × P(Y = 1) = f1 g6 + f6 g1 .

If P(X + Y = 2) = P(X + Y = 12) = P(X + Y = 7) = 1/11, then f1 g1 = f6 g6 (i.e. f6 /f1 = (g6 /g1 )−1 ) and also   1 g6 f6 1 = P(X + Y = 7) ≥ f1 g1 + = (x + x−1 ), 11 g1 f1 11

whereby x = g6 /g1 . However, for all x > 0, x + x−1 is always strictly greater than 1, hence a contradiction.

3 Random variables 3.1. We act sequentially as follows:

pX (1) = P(X = 1) = P(winner found in first round) = P(different items in first round) = 1 − P(same items in first round) = 1 − P(both rock or both paper or both scissors)  2 = 1 − 91 + 19 + 19 = ; then, using the fact that rounds are independent of each other:

pX (2) = P(X = 2) = P(winner found in second round) = P((same items in first round) ∩ (different items in second round)) = P(same items in first round) P(different items in second round) = 31 × 23

and

pX (3) = P(X = 3) = P(winner found in third round) = P((same items in first two rounds) ∩ (different items in third round)) = 13 × 31 × 23 .

From this pattern it is apparent that

1 x−1  2 pX (x) = 3 , x ∈ SX = {1, 2,}

3.2. The graph should be like:

• The curve is right skewed; • The density is 0 for x less than 40 minutes, and (almost) 0 for x greater than 120 minutes; • The curve should have its ‘centre of gravity’ close to 60; • The area under the curve less than 52 minutes is indicated to be 0.25; • One mode is expected. If a multi-modal distribution is proposed, plausible reasons for this should be given.

An example of such a density function is given below.

3.3. The sample space S consists of 24 = 16 equally likely outcomes.

(a) Let X be the number of heads before the first tail. What happens after the first tail is irrelevant, so we stop recording as soon as a tail appears (or after four heads if no tail appears). We compute P(X = x) by considering the first appearance of a tail: – X = 0: T... (tail on first flip): 1/2. – X = 1: H then T...: 1/2 · 1/2 = 1/4. – X = 2: HH then T...: (1/2)3 = 1/8. – X = 3: HHH then T: (1/2)4 = 1/16. – X = 4: HHHH (no tail): (1/2)4 = 1/16. So the pmf of X is: 1   2 x=0  x=1     4 1  x=2 pX (x) = P(X = x) = 1   x=3  1   x=4  0 otherwise 

(the domain of variation is SX = {0, 1, 2, 3, 4}) and the corresponding cdf is:    0 k<0  0≤x<1     2 3 1 ≤ x < 2  FX (x) = P(X ≤ x) = 47    8 2≤x<3 3≤x<4      1 x≥4 (step function, as X is evidently discrete).

(b) Let Y be the number of heads after the first tail. We go through each of the 16 sequences and count how many heads occur after the first tail (if any). If no tail appears, Y = 0. Enumerating all outcomes and counting yields the pmf:   y=0  y=1    16  pY (y) = P(Y = y) = 4 y=2  1 y=3     0 otherwise (the domain of variation is SY = {0, 1, 2, 3}) and the corresponding cdf is:    0 y<0   16 0 ≤ y < 1    FY (y) = P(Y ≤ y) = 11 16 1≤y<2  15 2≤y<3     1 y ≥ 3

(c) Let Z = H − T (where H is the number of heads and T is the number of tails). In 4 tosses, the number of heads H can be 0,1,2,3,4, and so Z = H − (4 − H) = 2H − 4. This gives the domain of variation SZ = {−4, −2, 0, 2, 4} for Z. The number of ways  to get h heads exactly is h (the binomial coefficient, ‘in how many different ways can we choose h objects out of 4’). As Z and H are in one-to-one correspondence, this gives directly the pmf of Z: 1  z = −4 (h = 0)  z = −2 (h = 1)    6  z = 0 (h = 2) P(Z = z) = 4   z = 2 (h = 3)  1  z = 4 (h = 4)    0 otherwise and the corresponding cdf:    0 z < −4  −4 ≤ z < −2    5  −2 ≤ z < 0 F (z) = P(Z ≤ z) = 11   0≤z<2 2≤z<4      1 z≥4

(d) Let W = H ·T . For 4 tosses, H ∈ {0, 1, 2, 3, 4} and T = 4−H. Then W = H(4−H). As in (c), this gives:

H W = H(4 − H) Probability 0 0 16 1 3 16 2 4 16 3 3 16 4 0 16

So in the long run, he shouldn’t lose any money. (b) i.  3 y=0  51   y=1 pY (y) = 1 y=2  5   0 otherwise ii. 3 1 1 E (Y ) = × 0 + × 1 + × 2 = 0.6 cents. 5 5 5 3.15. a) Let S(r) be the expected score achieved by following the strategy “stop the first time the die shows r or greater”. By elementary calculations, we can find

S(6) = 6 × P(6 appears before 1) + 1 × P(1 appears before 6) = 7/2,

as P(6 appears before 1) = P(1 appears before 6) = 1/2 (the events “6 appears before 1” and “1 appears before 6” form a partition of the sample space, and are equally likely). Similar arguments yield S(5) = 4, S(4) = 4, S(3) = 19/5 and S(2) = 7/2. The optimal strategy is therefore to stop at the first throw greater than or equal to 4. b) Similar calculations show that ‘stop at 5 or 6’ is now the best strategy to maximize the expected score. Compared to (a), the ‘reward’ is now much more important for 5 (score = 25) and 6 (score = 36) than for 4 (score = 16), so you’d better let a 4 go by and run the risk to play again.

3.16. Let X be the demand in items, with probability mass function

P(X = 2) = 0.1, P(X = 3) = 0.4 and P(X = 4) = 0.5.

Given that the demand will be minimum 2 and maximum 4, only stock values n = 2, 3, 4 must be considered. Let Pn be the profit when n items are stocked. Then, we can write

Pn = 12 min(X, n) − 10n = 12 min(X − n, 0) + 2n See that Yn = min(X − n, 0) is a random variable such that   0 if X ≥ n Yn = −1 if X = n − 1 −2 if X = n − 2 

so that its expectation is

E(Yn ) = −P(X = n − 1) − 2P(X = n − 2).

It follows   E(Pn ) = −12 × P(X = n − 1) + 2P(X = n − 2) + 2n Now, taking successively n = 2, n = 3 and n = 4, we find

E(P2 ) = 4, E(P3 ) = 4.8 and E(P4 ) = 0.8,

so that the expected profit is maximized for n = 3, with a maximal value of $4.8. 3.17. We have, for all s, X 1 s φX (s) = E esX = esx P(X = x) = e + e2s + e3s   x∈{1,2,3}
